chapters.title: A woman’s contribution can make this world a better place
chapters.text:
James Brown’s most famous song is “It’s a Man’s Man’s Man’s World.” Many people find truth in the lyrics claiming that the world belongs to men even if their deeds are devoted to women. This sexist and discriminating perspective limits the vision of what this world could be if both sexes contributed to progress and creativity.

Men have competed with women for centuries and often refused to play fair. Women’s vulnerability became a ground for men to advance and harass their female colleagues at work; Silicon Valley has silenced many scandals to save its reputation and money.

Male-centered attitudes and discrimination are why so many women cannot enter the technology industry and struggle to stay employed, facing further humiliation, sexism, and racism. For example, only 20% of Google tech workers in 2017 were women. Moreover, women abandon their technical jobs twice as fast as men because they feel unsafe in a hostile and discriminative environment.

Furthermore, women who want to start a company and need investments face male indifference, as some men still believe that women must cook, clean, and babysit, not rule the country, run a firm, or code. All professional spheres lack female employees, even though more women than men are graduating from college these days.

Mass culture and even Apple apps are designed to satisfy men’s needs while neglecting women’s.

This world needs another perspective to progress steadily and positively. Women must design artificial intelligence, construct buildings, advance technology, and introduce critical scientific changes as men do; otherwise, this world will remain “the man’s world,” with females living and working on the margins of progress and history.

In this summary, you’ll discover the nature and reasons for sexism and how it affects Silicon Valley, preventing women from the success and safety they deserve.

chapters.title: Unrecognized and marginalized scientists and programmers
chapters.text:
How would computer games look if more female developers worked in tech? What new apps and features would the iPhone have if Apple’s CEO was a woman? These questions may sound trivial, but they could have changed the world if we had answered them years ago. When Telle Whitney started college in 1973, she had no idea she would end up in the tech world. When she tried programming at the University of Utah, she enjoyed it immensely. However, the awkward atmosphere in the classroom complicated her studies. She was the only female student in her class. Her classmates and professors struggled to treat her as a female professional. Such an environment forced Whitney to hide her womanly curves in oversized T-shirts to ease the tension and make communication with her colleagues more comfortable.

This example is one of many where men set the rules for what is acceptable and comfortable based on their views; women must accommodate and make necessary changes to continue their work or studies. In the end, few women receive the deserved recognition. For example, six chosen women were the first to program the ENIAC, the first U.S. computer. However, the press did not mention any of the female scientists when describing the new technology and its creators in 1946.

Female scientists were the first programmers in history. While men focused on the manual part of engineering, women worked on the software of the soon-to-be computers. The harmony didn’t last long, though, as in the 1960s, the tech industry went from science to magic. Many CEOs believed that a candidate’s traits directly affected the quality of their work. Therefore, they selected the employee not only by their degree or knowledge but also by their sex. Somehow, the image of a professional coder acquired a male shape.

The tech sphere would be more diverse and progressive if men cooperated with women instead of competing with them.

One of the primary reasons men shut women off was the belief that programmers were supposed to be introverts. Many couldn’t imagine a woman sacrificing leisure and communication for their work. Thus, women couldn't make good programmers because their personalities didn’t correspond to the profile of a “perfectly happy programmer.”

Unfortunately, this stereotype persists today. Many expect coders to be timid, antisocial, geeky, and … male.

chapters.title: Being in the minority turns women into easy targets
chapters.text:
Technology is the leading industry in the U.S., where women are practically excluded. Among 150 companies with nine-digit worth, only two have a female founder. Peter Theil, the co-founder of PayPal, recognizes that the gender imbalance wouldn’t be so powerful and evident if investors supported more female founders. However, he has no clue how to solve the issue.

Silicon Valley has its rules and principles, but who sets them? Usually, wealthy men. Most capitalists invest in businesses run by men because they trust those who resemble them in behavior, outlook, and sex. Moreover, many men believe the industry doesn’t need diversity — it needs guys like them to make money and follow the standard rules; hiring women would be unnecessary trouble.

When Peter Theil and Max Levchin were starting PayPal, they hired males with the same views and intentions, and when they employed a woman, she stayed only for a few weeks. The alpha-male attitude in the company made it impossible for her to feel comfortable and safe. The male environment didn’t recognize any mistakes, yet we know that where there are no errors, there is no growth.

Furthermore, gender imbalance can shatter the harmonious atmosphere in the workplace even when women are employed. For example, Google’s staff has always been male-dominated. However, it strived to hire a diverse team by giving power to several notably strong and intelligent women. One of them was Susan Wojcicki, employed to be their marketing manager; she did very well, propelling Google to greater heights. While Susan was not a programmer, she was a crucial figure in the success of Google. Thanks to her, the company turned into an international tech giant.

Women’s value didn’t go unnoticed, and Google created a team to facilitate female recruitment into the organization. Eventually, more and more females worked in HR, marketing, and other non-tech divisions. Nonetheless, men dominate all Google divisions, while women are a significant minority. Such a situation makes females easy victims of unprofessional treatment, discrimination, and sexual harassment. Therefore, the working environment aggravates the career path for many women as they must ignore overt flirting, humiliation, and objectivism to succeed professionally.

Women’s compassion and creativity can diversify and enhance business.

Did you know? A Zippia article in November 2022 revealed that 54% to 81% of women report experiencing sexual harassment at work: from catcalls to unwelcome sexual advances or touching.

chapters.title: Sexual harassment in the workplace forces women to job switch
chapters.text:
Many men often make suggestive comments or unwelcome sexual advances, but many people remain blind to it, especially regarding women in the workplace. Often, females feel alone and helpless because they have no support. Revealing cases of abuse may cost them their jobs, and sometimes, the situation may have a worse outcome. If the accusation lies on someone in power, and the woman has no evidence or support, the authorities can turn allegations against her.

Support is crucial for a victory.

Susan Fowler was an engineer at Uber. She posted her experience of sexual harassment in the company, and the information went viral, receiving a massive reaction. Fowler stayed silent throughout the tide until she spoke to the New York Times seven months later and revealed what she went through.

Despite the support, she also received a backlash from people, especially men, who claimed she was overreacting. People trolled and threatened her, saying she had no right to complain.

Getting to fifty-fifty is incredibly complex and nuanced, requiring many detailed solutions that will take decades to fully play out. ~ Emily Chang

While many argue that women create the ground for men’s advancement, we must examine female motivation to enter a potentially dangerous or unpleasant environment with male colleagues. Whenever a male coworker asks a woman to go for drinks, the latter feels torn between accepting and declining. Most realize that missing out on after-work activities can make them outsiders in the workplace. As a result, their male colleagues can move up the career ladder much faster if they befriend their superiors. Yet, women may seem light-headed if they agree to participate; men may consider their consent as a green light to flirting or sexual advancement.

An unhealthy workplace affects a company's progress.

Sexual harassment is particularly widespread in a workplace that is already unhealthy. For example, some teams and divisions have leads and superiors that enjoy substances and inappropriate behavior during work time. They invite others to a strip club or bar after work. Such a dilemma makes women outcasts, hinders their professional growth, reduces job satisfaction, and leads to psychological issues. Therefore, many women leave their job even if they like it, and put up with a less profitable or exciting career, only to feel safe and appreciated professionally.

chapters.title: The business world is another boy’s club
chapters.text:
It’s challenging to tell from someone’s background whether they’ll be successful in the venture business, but that’s precisely what tech giants do to women. Michael Moritz, one of the most prominent investors in Silicon Valley, made a controversial statement about his company’s employment policy; he didn’t hire women because there were no sufficiently talented, educated, and professionally capable employees among women. Moritz said he wouldn’t lower the company’s demands to meet women’s capabilities. Through that statement, Moritz disregarded efforts women have been putting in every sphere possible and expressed his disbelief about the female career potential in the present and future. Gender imbalance and power struggles aren't new. Women had to pave the path alone as fathers and husbands rarely supported their beloved wives and daughters in their careers. Nonetheless, even if women did succeed, very few people recognized their contribution; men still owned and inherited everything legally. Therefore, starting a business even now is a huge challenge for women as their sex may be the principal obstacle on the way to their dream.

In the business world, acquaintances can be decisive.

Successful venture capitalists form a network where everyone knows everyone else; it’s like a boy’s club where no girl is allowed. Unfortunately, this metaphor is effective even now. Men support and rely on other men because of gender, not trustworthiness. They head 92% of businesses that depend on investments.

However, investors can find talented, determined, educated, and resilient women to bring them significant profits only if they ignore their gender and don’t let it distort their vision. Even if a woman finds investments for a startup, she will still struggle to collect more money when her business is running and expanding. Thus, a woman can’t conquer the industry without connections or influential friends.

Society encourages men to win and allows them to lose. Women can enjoy neither.

Sexism affects women at all levels, setting limitations in their minds, environment, and professional growth. Therefore, many women have to play it small while their male counterparts conquer the market. Women take smaller loans and focus on building a small but sustainable business as they have more chances on a lower scale.

chapters.title: Sexual endeavors must happen in the bedroom, not a workplace
chapters.text:
As the world is coming to terms with the rights of expression and individualism, activities outside of the workplace don’t stay there anymore. The sexual revolution in the form of excessive partying, orgies, escorts, and “sugar-baby” services is becoming increasingly widespread among wealthy men. Silicon Valley boys leave work to relax in another strip club. Such a lifestyle affects men’s perception of all women, including their female co-workers. The money boosts men’s self-worth and confidence, making them believe they can buy anything, including female attention and sexual openness. Furthermore, some women encourage this stereotype by becoming the receiving end of such a transactional relationship: they escort men, satisfy their needs, and accept money for their services.

Stories of harassment and discrimination live on in whispers or are simply never told. ~ Emily Chang

Grave problems arise when the parties and sexually open behavior spill into the workplace. These dirty mixed scenarios are degrading, humiliating, and even dangerous for female workers. It can be even more aggravating when a man’s position is substantially higher and his moral principles lower than the woman’s. It forms a perfect ground for sexual harassment or even abuse.

The misconception that women can do anything for a check leads men to misbehave and treat their female colleagues as pretty objects or the fairer sex, not as teammates. Therefore, sexism and amorality destroy a healthy and harmonious environment essential for a company’s growth.

Alcohol and parties lead to a hangover, not professional success.

Frivolity in working relationships can be dangerous as men can read the signs wrongly and thus justify their sexual advancement. As a result, women feel left out in male-dominated teams, as they cannot befriend their colleagues unless they are women. Nevertheless, this approach can save a woman from getting tricked into a false connection or a relationship. Thus, men leave women with terrible alternatives: loneliness and isolation or danger of sexual harassment and abuse.

A woman must observe her male colleague’s words and behavior before giving her trust. Business work can be challenging; it has many pitfalls, lies, and sexism. Therefore, society must strive to build a safer, more professional, and gender-balanced working environment where all female employees can feel protected and appreciated for their skills and talents, not their looks or sex.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Like many other industries, the tech industry is mainly unfair to women. From being sidelined to being taken advantage of, women have struggled to make headway in the male-dominated Silicon Valley. While the industry has become more inclusive, it’s still far from being safe for female employees.

The first step to bridging this gap is to accept that there’s a striking gender imbalance. The majority of people do not accept this disparity. They claim they would hire more women if there were more women programmers, but that’s far from the truth. Limitations appear before a girl even gets to school. Society determines what is right and wrong for a female of any age. Men’s power is undeniable in the business and tech world, where males have reigned for decades, determining who can succeed and who can fail. Women who love programming and dream of building a company will face considerable obstacles, as only some men support female startups and professional endeavors.

Thus, society must reconsider its values and understand that women can match or surpass the levels attained by men. We should also promote women as great leaders, founders, and superiors, not just subordinates. Business people must embrace diversity and promote inclusion at all levels. Thus we can eliminate all barriers and build a relationship that thrives on intellectual capacity and teamwork.

Our actions today determine the future we’ll create for the coming generations. So, we must honestly assess the current problems and work hard to overcome them so that our daughters enjoy comfort, safety, and freedom in their work, home, and life.

Try this

Encourage women to actively participate in technical professions and learn about investments, businesses, and innovation. Promoting successful women on social media can motivate girls to follow their dreams of coding or running a business.

Read it till the end?
